# Questions in Livestream 246

Live Stream #246: Egyptian Royal Streaming

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=466" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:46</a> | Are they going to make the second book into an audiobook? | Probably not unless sales improve, because the first audiobook sold very poorly relative to the work involved. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=620" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:20</a> | Can the pharaonic dynasty be traced down to the present day? | No; he says the native dynastic line ended long ago, and there is no surviving royal succession to trace forward. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=779" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:59</a> | What temple-cultic language appears in Genesis 1-11 besides creation and the ark? | He points to image-of-God statuary themes, temple-court imagery, sacrifice, and the offerings of Cain, Abel, and Noah. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=933" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:33</a> | Does the table of presence go back in concept to creation? | In one sense yes, because it is a perpetual offering and offerings themselves go back to the earliest chapters of Genesis. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=1042" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:22</a> | Why do none of the three most central creeds say "I believe in the Bible"? | Timing and context: complete Bible codices were not yet the iconic common object people imagine, and the creeds were aimed at disputed doctrines rather than at an authority nobody in the church was denying. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=1124" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:44</a> | What about Codex Vaticanus and Alexandrinus? | He says such manuscripts existed, but they were rare, expensive, not copies of one another, and not what ordinary Christians or churches usually had available. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=1280" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:20</a> | What is monolithic monotheism again? | He defines it as belief in one personal God without distinct divine persons, giving Islam as an example and contrasting it with monarchial monotheism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=1394" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:14</a> | If intelligent extraterrestrials exist, are humans supposed to expand through the universe and bring them the gospel? | He says humanity is commanded to fill the earth, not the universe, so space evangelization is not a biblical imperative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=1537" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:37</a> | In Genesis, when Jacob wrestled with God, is there an interpretation that he actually wrestled with a demon? | He does not see how that reading works, apart from someone over-reading the fact that Jacob was wounded. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=1811" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:11</a> | Why is no one in exodus studies engaging Shlomit Bechar's Hazor work the way some engage Nigro's work? | He says some people do engage it, including him, and that Bechar's work especially undercuts the claim of a 15th-century Hazor destruction layer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=2296" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:16</a> | Why is Jacob named Israel again in Genesis 35:10 after already being renamed earlier? | He treats Genesis 35 as a reaffirmation in a new setting rather than a contradictory second first-naming. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=3162" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:42</a> | Could a name in Ramesses I's Amara West topographical list be Jericho as proposed by Grzybek? | He says it is possible but not very likely; the better reading probably points somewhere in Syria, and the proposal has not gained much traction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=3299" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:59</a> | Does Genesis 47:26 imply Joseph's 20% tax was still in place by Dynasty 19? | Yes; he says the verse itself points that way and notes the tax continued much later in Egyptian history as well. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=3422" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:02</a> | Could Merneptah have indirectly taken credit for an Israelite defeat or setback without directly encountering Israel? | No. He says Egyptian victory texts were religious offerings, so a king could not simply claim a victory he had not actually performed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=3750" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:30</a> | Does the reference to spoked wheels in an Amenemhat II inscription show knowledge of wheeled devices in Dynasty 12? | Yes, but as imported technology from Canaan rather than a fully native Egyptian wheeled tradition at that point. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=3924" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:24</a> | Does 1 Peter 3:21 teach that baptism saves? | He treats the verse in the context of the gospel and argues it should not be read as making the water rite itself the saving cause. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=4213" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:13</a> | Does he think Aristotle is in heaven? | He does not know; he says it would depend on Aristotle's relation to Christ, while allowing that pre-Christian people could not respond to Christ in the same explicit way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=4336" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:16</a> | What is worship? Is it just singing? | No; he defines worship as ascribing worth to God and Christ, which can include music but is broader than music. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=4495" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:55</a> | Do declarative praise psalms count as worship? | Yes; he says they can count as worship because worship can include any medium that ascribes glory and honor to God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=4594" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:34</a> | Does fasting during the day while feasting at night count as worship? | Not really; he says fasting by itself is not the same thing as worship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=4622" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:02</a> | What will happen to tithing as worship if cash is illegal? | He says preserving the ritual aspect of giving becomes harder without cash or checks, but other tangible payment forms may still be possible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=4832" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:32</a> | Is 1 John 1:8-10 about initial justification or ongoing repentance in a Christian's life? | He reads it as the ongoing believing life: believers continue walking in the light, confessing sin, and being cleansed rather than claiming sinless perfection. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=5227" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:07</a> | Is walking in darkness willful, deliberate sin? | It can be, but he says it can also be drift or iniquity, where a believer is distracted or misdirected and ends up walking in darkness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=5479" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:19</a> | Is walking in darkness a disruption in a believer's relationship with God that can lead to apostasy if unchecked? | Yes; he says walking in darkness cuts connection with God and requires believers to examine whether their walk matches their profession. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=5675" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:35</a> | What other Egyptologist scholars does he recommend for reading about the Exodus besides himself, Kitchen, and Hoffmeier? | He recommends Mark Janzen as someone who has done good work, though not a large amount, on the Exodus. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=5723" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:23</a> | Does the Septuagint preserve the authentic version of Jeremiah? | He is cautious; he says the Septuagint preserves the version its translators had, but that does not necessarily make a shorter or longer version original. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=5939" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:59</a> | In Genesis 11, was the tower and city of Babel destroyed or just abandoned when God scattered the people? | The text does not say; he thinks it was probably an ordinary ziggurat that may have been abandoned, left unfinished, or ruined later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=6019" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:19</a> | Why is there debate about Babel being a ziggurat if that seems obvious? | He says Western art shaped many imaginations by depicting Babel as a massive tower rising toward heaven rather than as an ordinary ziggurat. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=6303" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:03</a> | Has he read *Refuting Compromise*, and how does he answer people who call him liberal for not believing Genesis days must be 24-hour periods? | He has not read it, and he says rejecting a 24-hour-day view is not liberalism because it does not deny central Christian doctrines. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=6444" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:24</a> | What is happening in Deuteronomy 23:28-29? | He says he cannot answer because there is no such passage in English Bibles. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=6543" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:03</a> | In dating the Exodus, how much weight does he put on the dating of the conquest? | He says the conquest dating confirms his Exodus dating, with a sequence from the Exodus through the initial conquest and Shiloh fitting the late-date model. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=6638" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:38</a> | Is gluttony the first sin? | No; he says gluttony is not mere overeating but taking someone else's rightful share, which is why the ancient world treated it so seriously. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=6893" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:53</a> | What tips does he have for writing an alternate-world fantasy novel with an 18th- or 19th-century archaeology subplot? | He says early archaeology was more like treasure hunting, so writers should read old field reports and capture the period's museum focus and problematic attitudes toward locals. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=7408" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:28</a> | Has he made his own Bible timeline, and can viewers see it? | Not a full one yet; he says he is still working on a peer-reviewed patriarchal timeline and does not want to release it prematurely. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=7477" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:37</a> | Is Ezekiel 29 a failed prophecy about Egypt? | He says no; he reads the passage as promising Nebuchadnezzar loot from Egypt as wages for his army, not permanent conquest of the land. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=7815" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:15</a> | Has he heard of a book claiming stylometry proves the New Testament is second century and Paul did not exist? | He has not read the book, but he rejects stylometry as junk science and says Paul's existence and first-century writings are not credibly denied. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=7994" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:14</a> | Would he consider academic cage-match style debate collaborations after the Faulk versus Kandy exchange? | No; he does not like doing debates and would rather teach and write than spend time on debate showmanship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=8078" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:14:38</a> | Is the name Nimrod related or cognate to Ninurta, and could that fit a literary amalgamation? | He says Nimrod is clearly a literary amalgamation, but he is doubtful about deriving the name from Ninurta because the consonant shift seems unlikely. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=8167" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:16:07</a> | Are literary amalgamations common? | Yes; he says they are common in Israelite literature and gives examples from both the Old and New Testaments. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=8396" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:56</a> | In the offering of Isaac, is there a burnt-offering aspect to Christ's sacrifice, and could the fire be the wrath of God? | He warns not to make typology walk on all fours; the ram can be a type of Christ without every detail, such as the fire, mapping onto Christ. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=8640" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:00</a> | How can a homeschool co-op be persuaded to allow a logic fallacies and debate class? | He thinks the deeper problem is educational quality and fear of qualified teachers, and suggests outside evaluation or accreditation-style review rather than only arguing for one class. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=9849" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:09</a> | Why is God pictured as an old gray-haired man when the ideal age is 25 to 35? | He says this is an artistic way to convey God's title "Ancient of Days" and divine antiquity, not a claim that God is literally an old man. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=10066" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:47:46</a> | What did Ramesses II do after the Exodus? | He says Ramesses continued reigning for decades, completed little more building, lost many sons over time, and saw Egypt's power begin to weaken. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=10368" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:48</a> | Why did the Egyptians not begin to worship Yahweh after the Exodus if their gods could not stop him? | He says adopting Yahweh would have required a deep metaphysical shift from polytheism to monotheism, and Egyptians may also have seen Yahweh as leaving with Israel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=10704" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:24</a> | Does the Bible say whether non-delta Egyptian polytheists knew where or from whom the ten plagues originated? | He says the Bible does not say, and upper Egyptians may have experienced some plagues, such as the Nile turning to blood, without knowing all their origin. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=10828" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:00:28</a> | Did the Egyptians' metaphysical framework radically change when they became Christian? | He says the framework had already been shifting through later Egyptian religion, Greco-Roman influence, and Jewish presence before Christianity arrived. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=10999" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:19</a> | Why did God use plagues and disasters against the king of Egypt? | He says the plagues were meant to get the people's attention and show Egypt was on the wrong course, while also pressuring the king. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=11166" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:06:06</a> | Besides Kenyon's Jericho excavation volumes, what other books are worth searching for on archive.org? | He does not list specific titles, but recommends using Egypt Exploration Foundation resources and notes that archive.org has many older Egyptology and archaeology works. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=11248" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:07:28</a> | Can someone be sued for calling another person a liar? | He says not usually for a vague accusation, but a false, specific, malicious, and damaging claim can become a defamation issue; he notes he is not giving legal advice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=11381" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:41</a> | What is God's relationship to the material, and would anything existing alongside God be ontologically equal to God? | He says God must be transcendent from creation; otherwise monotheism collapses into pantheism or panentheism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=11551" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12:31</a> | Was Ian Gillan correct when he said there is no laughing in heaven? | No; he says heaven includes joy, happiness, and humor, even if not spiteful mockery. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=11942" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:19:02</a> | Is free will only for this world, with no free choice to sin in heaven? | He says people do not lose free will in heaven; rather, they know the cost of sin and will not choose it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=12043" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:43</a> | Does sin require a corporeal framework that is absent in heaven? | No; he points to fallen spiritual beings as examples that sin is not limited to corporeal existence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=12175" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:22:55</a> | What would he do if Canada passed a law allowing arrests over religious beliefs? | He says the bill in question had been tabled, and if such a law passed he would keep doing the livestream and let the consequences fall where they may. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=12530" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:50</a> | Was Chalcedon wrong to excommunicate the miaphysites? | He says that is somewhat anachronistic because the category was not yet developed; Coptic representatives may have sounded monophysite then, even though later theology was refined. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=13132" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:38:52</a> | Why do many Copts and Middle Eastern Christians seem complacent with Islam? | He says they are a minority under real social pressure and often must coexist carefully, though some still evangelize within legal and personal risks. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=13249" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:40:49</a> | Can Copts rebuild churches in Egypt if classical Sharia would restrict rebuilding places of worship? | He says Egypt is not governed by Sharia in that way, so Copts can rebuild churches, though they may not receive much protection from attacks. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=13740" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:49:00</a> | Do Copts use Sahidic, Bohairic, or another dialect as their liturgical language? | He is not sure and thinks it may be Sahidic, but explicitly says not to quote him on that. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=13803" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:50:03</a> | Are there Christian converts in Egypt? | Yes, but he says conversion from Islam remains legally fraught, so converts often stay quiet about it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=13920" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:52:00</a> | Why do people think finding the Ark of the Covenant would force people to admit God is real rather than just prove Hebrews built a ritual box? | He says finding it would not force faith; radical skepticism can always narrow the evidence and explain it away. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fXqKmfztALQ?t=14173" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:56:13</a> | In light of Ephesians 2:14-16, is it difficult to eliminate pagan elements in church history from the Gentiles? | He says not inherently; pagan accretions should be discerned case by case, since some are harmful, some harmless, and some may even support the faith. |
